name: Talavera de la Reina on bike lane – circular route
name_original: Talavera de la Reina en carril bici – ruta circular
region_intro: Touring cycling around Cazalegas offers diverse landscapes, centered around the prominent Cazalegas Reservoir on the Alberche River. The region features a "serrano" landscape characterized by holm oaks, oaks, and cork oaks, with olive groves and vineyards along riverbanks. Cyclists encounter varied terrain, including flat sections near the reservoir and more challenging climbs towards viewpoints offering panoramic views of the Sierra de San Vicente and Gredos mountain range. The area provides a mix of paved roads and unpaved tracks, suitable for different cycling preferences. Best touring cycling routes around Cazalegas The most popular touring cycling route is Talavera de la Reina on bike lane – circular route, a 19.0 miles (30.6 km) trail that takes 1 hour 49 minutes to complete. This easy route features a dedicated bike lane. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Segurilla Watchtower – La Portiña Reservoir loop from La Cantina, a difficult 30.3 miles (48.8 km) path. This route includes significant elevation gain and passes by the La Portiña Reservoir. Local touring cyclists also love the Cazalegas Reservoir – Puerto del Piélago loop from Cazalegas, a 37.2 miles (59.8 km) trail leading through varied terrain including the Cazalegas Reservoir, often completed in about 4 hours 16 minutes. Touring cycling around Cazalegas is defined by the Cazalegas Reservoir, the Alberche River, and the "serrano" landscape with holm oaks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy paths to challenging climbs. The routes in Cazalegas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 1,100 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Cazalegas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Cazalegas?
A: There are over 130 touring cycling routes available in and around Cazalegas, catering to various skill levels. The komoot community has explored more than 1,100 of these routes, highlighting the area's popularity among cyclists.

Q: What are the typical difficulty levels for touring cycling routes in Cazalegas?
A: Cazalegas offers a good mix of routes for all abilities. You'll find 22 easy routes, 37 moderate routes, and 76 difficult routes, ensuring there's a suitable option whether you're looking for a relaxed ride or a challenging adventure.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Cazalegas?
A: Yes, for a family-friendly option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1030619709/talavera-de-la-reina-on-bike-lane-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Talavera de la Reina on bike lane – circular route&lt;/a&gt;. This easy 30.6 km trail features a dedicated bike lane, making it a safe and enjoyable ride for all ages. The area around the Cazalegas Reservoir also offers flatter sections suitable for a relaxed pace.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Cazalegas?
A: The terrain around Cazalegas is quite varied. You'll encounter flat sections, especially near the Cazalegas Reservoir and along the Alberche River, which are ideal for relaxed rides. However, many routes also feature more challenging climbs, particularly as you head towards viewpoints offering panoramic views of the Sierra de San Vicente and the Gredos mountain range. Expect a mix of paved roads and unpaved tracks, passing through landscapes characterized by holm oaks, olive groves, and vineyards.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Cazalegas?
A: Yes, many routes in Cazalegas are designed as loops. An example of a challenging circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20139689" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazalegas Reservoir – Puerto del Piélago loop from Cazalegas&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 59.8 km and leads through varied terrain, including the reservoir area.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: The most prominent natural feature is the Cazalegas Reservoir, built on the Alberche River, which many routes pass by or around. You can also enjoy views of the Sierra de San Vicente and the Gredos mountain range. Along the routes, you might encounter cultural points of interest such as the Mudejar-style Church of San Vicente Mártir in Cazalegas or the 16th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5013825" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Talavera de la Reina&lt;/a&gt;, a nearby settlement with historical significance. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4966496" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castilla-La Mancha Bridge&lt;/a&gt; is another notable landmark.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Cazalegas?
A: The touring cycling routes in Cazalegas are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.1 stars from over 50 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, the scenic views of the reservoir and mountains, and the variety of terrain that caters to different skill levels.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by reservoirs other than Cazalegas?
A: Yes, for a route that includes another significant body of water, consider the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40325140" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Segurilla Watchtower – La Portiña Reservoir loop from La Cantina&lt;/a&gt;. This 48.8 km path features significant elevation gain and passes by the La Portiña Reservoir, offering additional scenic views.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Cazalegas?
A: Cazalegas is suitable for cycling for much of the year. The spring and autumn months generally offer pleasant temperatures for touring. While summers can be warm, the Alberche River provides a refreshing escape, and routes along the reservoir can be enjoyable. Always check local weather conditions before heading out.

Q: Are there any routes with significant elevation gain for advanced cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For advanced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40325701" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dehesa Landscape – La Portiña Reservoir loop from Parking Salones Prado del Arca&lt;/a&gt; offer substantial elevation gain, with over 620 meters of ascent over 48.4 km. These routes provide a demanding workout amidst beautiful scenery.

Q: Can I find routes that explore the typical 'serrano' landscape of the region?
A: Many routes will immerse you in the characteristic 'serrano' landscape of Cazalegas, featuring holm oaks, oaks, and cork oaks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40326252" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Water Spout – Puente de los Molinos loop from Cardiel de los Montes&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, takes you through this type of terrain, offering a true taste of the local natural environment.
tips: In 1982, a small plot of land was expanded and added to the perimeter of the Jardines del Prado, La Alameda, a garden built in the English style, with a central lake and two bridges completely covered with traditional Talavera ceramics.

It extends along the eastern bank of the Tagus River and is the second most populated municipality in the province and the fourth in Castilla-La Mancha.
Among its monuments are the Basilica of Our Lady of the Prado, which houses what is today the patron saint of the city. It was called Queen of the Hermitages by Philip II, it is also called the Sistine Chapel of ceramics, because this colorful art shows here all its splendor from the 16th century to the present day. The style of its construction is a combination of Renaissance and Baroque, although it has undergone various restorations over the centuries. It was elevated to the dignity of Basilica in 1989 by Pope John Paul II.
In the Church of Santa María La Mayor (La Colegial) you can admire its pure Gothic cloister, which was built around the year 1469. Its floor plan is rectangular, made up of three longitudinal naves and without a transept. In the years 1704 and 1705 the two upper sections of the Tower were added.

The new Talavera de la Reina cable-stayed bridge is an engineering prodigy, it is a world record in the type of cable-stayed bridges with a single mast in high-resistance prestressed concrete, with the highest inclined pylon in Europe. Its 152 stays and 192 meters make it the second highest cable-stayed bridge in Spain and the second in Europe. It is a display of national and international architecture.
The merino cord is an ancient livestock route that was used for the transit of sheep of this breed between populations. Today it has a bike lane that connects with Talavera de la Reina.

waypoints.name: Prado Gardens and La Alameda (Talavera de la Reina)
